narrative: Your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 stands out as the single most influential factor in the model's assessment, working in your favor by suggesting a stronger likelihood of passing the bar exam compared to what might be expected otherwise—it's below the 3.24 average among students who passed, but it still pulled the prediction toward success more than any other element. Next in importance was your Year 3 decile ranking of 2, which unfortunately pushed the prediction toward failure, as it's notably lower than the 5.91 average for those who passed, signaling to the model potential challenges in sustaining higher performance. Your middle family income bracket also played a supportive role, nudging the outlook toward passing since it aligns with the profile of many who succeed, though it's less common among top performers than upper-middle or high incomes. Overall, these factors led the model to predict a 40% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which crossed the threshold for rejecting your law school application to prioritize candidates with lower predicted risks. Your LSAT score of 25 and Year 1 decile of 2 further highlight areas where you're below typical admitted students' levels, like the 37.27 LSAT average for passers, contributing to the cautious decision. Being full-time is a plus, matching the vast majority of successful students, but it couldn't outweigh the key drags. Compared to those typically accepted, your profile shows solid potential in GPA and background but lags in standardized metrics and later-year rankings that the model weighs heavily for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your abilities or background—gender and race weren't factors here—but a data-driven call based on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score toward the mid-30s range seen in passers, and focus on strategies to improve your class rankings, perhaps through tutoring or study groups. Building a stronger narrative around your undergraduate performance with explanations or recent achievements could also help in appeals or reapplying. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by targeting these gaps, so you're far from out of options. Keep pushing; your GPA shows you have the foundation to build on.
